annotations.label: Geometric series

anchor.snippet: a geometric series is a series summing the terms of an infinite geometric sequence

annotations.note: Mathlib has no standalone `GeometricSeries` type, but the series `Ōłæ x^n` is encoded via `formalMultilinearSeries_geometric` and via `Ōłæ' n, x^n` in summability lemmas.

annotations.label: Geometric mean property of terms

anchor.snippet: Each term in a geometric series is the geometric mean of the term before it and the term after it

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma was found stating that each term `a r^n` is the geometric mean of its neighbors `a r^(n-1)` and `a r^(n+1)`.

annotations.label: Geometric progression and common ratio

anchor.snippet: A geometric progression is a sequence obtained from an initial term, producing the next term by multiplying it by a constant from the previous term

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define `GeometricProgression` as a named structure; `fun n => a * r^n` is used directly without a dedicated abstraction.

annotations.label: Finite geometric series

anchor.snippet: Summing all terms above produces a finite geometric series

annotations.note: The finite geometric sum `Ōłæ i Ōłł range n, x^i` is the central object of `Mathlib/Algebra/Ring/GeomSum.lean` and `Mathlib/Algebra/Field/GeomSum.lean`.

annotations.label: Closed form for finite geometric series sum

anchor.snippet: the sum of a finite geometric series

annotations.note: `geom_sum_eq` states `Ōłæ i Ōłł range n, x^i = (x^n - 1)/(x - 1)` for `x ŌēĀ 1` in a field.

annotations.label: Infinite geometric series

anchor.snippet: When there are infinitely many terms in a geometric progression, the geometric series is

annotations.note: The infinite geometric series is captured as `Ōłæ' n, ╬Š^n` with summability/value lemmas like `hasSum_geometric_of_norm_lt_one` and `tsum_geometric_of_norm_lt_one`.

annotations.label: Grandi's series as divergent geometric series

anchor.snippet: Grandi's series is a divergent geometric series

annotations.note: A search for `Grandi` in Mathlib returned no matches; the divergence of `Ōłæ (-1)^n` is not stated as a named result.

annotations.label: Repeated decimal as geometric series

anchor.snippet: the repeated decimal fraction

annotations.note: `Real.ofDigits` formalizes `0.dŌéĆdŌéüdŌéé... = Ōłæ' n, dŌéÖ/b^(n+1)` via a geometric-like sum, but there is no dedicated lemma identifying repeating decimals with a geometric-series closed form.

annotations.label: Convergence criterion by common ratio magnitude

anchor.snippet: the terms of the series approach zero (becoming smaller and smaller in magnitude) and the sequence of partial sums

annotations.note: `summable_geometric_iff_norm_lt_one` characterizes summability of `╬Š^n` exactly by `ŌĆ¢╬ŠŌĆ¢ < 1`.

annotations.label: Root of unity ratio gives periodic partial sums

anchor.snippet: When the common ratio is a root of unity

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma states periodicity of geometric partial sums when `r` is a root of unity.

annotations.label: Rate of convergence of geometric series

anchor.snippet: The rate of convergence shows how the sequence quickly approaches its limit

annotations.note: No specific Mathlib statement about the rate (`|r|^n` decay) of geometric-series convergence as a named result was found.

annotations.label: Pattern of convergence by sign/argument

anchor.snippet: The pattern of convergence also depends on the sign or complex argument of the common ratio

annotations.note: No Mathlib statement classifies the qualitative convergence pattern (monotone vs alternating vs spiraling) of geometric series by `arg r`.

annotations.label: Closed-form partial sum formula

anchor.snippet: The partial sum of the first

annotations.note: `geom_sum_eq` gives `Ōłæ i Ōłł range n, x^i = (x^n - 1)/(x - 1)`, the standard closed-form partial sum.

annotations.label: Absolute convergence of infinite geometric series

anchor.snippet: the absolute value of r must be less than one for this sequence of partial sums to converge to a limit

annotations.note: `hasSum_geometric_of_norm_lt_one` states `HasSum (╬Š^┬Ę) (1-╬Š)Ōü╗┬╣` when `ŌĆ¢╬ŠŌĆ¢ < 1`, which is absolute convergence in normed fields.

annotations.label: Geometric series as a power series

anchor.snippet: The geometric series can therefore be considered a class of power series in which the sequence of coefficients satisfies

annotations.note: `formalMultilinearSeries_geometric` packages `1 + x + x┬▓ + ŌĆ”` as a `FormalMultilinearSeries`, i.e. a power series.

annotations.label: Radius of convergence equals 1

anchor.snippet: As a power series, the geometric series has a radius of convergence of 1

annotations.note: `formalMultilinearSeries_geometric_radius` proves `(formalMultilinearSeries_geometric ­ØĢ£ A).radius = 1` under `NormOneClass A`.

annotations.label: Koch snowflake area via geometric series

anchor.snippet: the geometric series may also be applied in finding the Koch snowflake

annotations.note: A search for `Koch`/`snowflake` only turned up `Mathlib/Topology/MetricSpace/Snowflaking.lean` (a different concept); the Koch snowflake area is not formalized.

annotations.label: Zeno's paradox of walking

anchor.snippet: in order to walk from one place to another, one must first walk half the distance there

annotations.note: Zeno's dichotomy as a worked example is not in Mathlib, although `tsum_geometric_two : Ōłæ' n, (1/2)^n = 2` provides the underlying identity.

annotations.label: Euclid Elements Book IX Proposition 35

anchor.snippet: If there is any multitude whatsoever of continually proportional numbers

annotations.note: Euclid IX.35 in modern form is `(Ōłæ i Ōłł range n, x^i)┬Ę(x - 1) = x^n - 1`, which is exactly `geom_sum_mul`.

annotations.label: Archimedes' parabola quadrature

anchor.snippet: Archimedes' theorem states that the total area under the parabola

annotations.note: A grep for `Archimedes`/`parabola` found no formalization of the parabola area = (4/3)┬Ętriangle result.

annotations.label: Two-dimensional geometric series decomposition

anchor.snippet: The first dimension is horizontal, in the bottom row, representing the geometric series

annotations.note: This pedagogical 2D geometric-series picture is not formalized as a Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Oresme's Gabriel's Staircase sum

anchor.snippet: Nicole Oresme

annotations.note: `hasSum_coe_mul_geometric_of_norm_lt_one` formalizes `Ōłæ n ┬Ę r^n = r/(1-r)┬▓` for `ŌĆ¢rŌĆ¢ < 1`, which specializes (at `r = 1/2`) to Oresme's `Ōłæ n/2^n = 2`, but Oresme's exact case is not a named lemma.

annotations.label: Geometric series over rings, fields, semirings

anchor.snippet: it is natural, in the context of modern algebra , to define geometric series with parameters from any ring or field

annotations.note: `geom_sum_mul` and the rest of `Mathlib/Algebra/Ring/GeomSum.lean` develop the finite geometric series over an arbitrary (commutative) ring.

annotations.label: 2-adic convergence of geometric series

anchor.snippet: in the convergence of the series

annotations.note: The general `summable_geometric_of_norm_lt_one` for `HasSummableGeomSeries` normed rings instantiates to the p-adic case, but no specific 2-adic geometric-series example is recorded.

annotations.label: Non-commutative geometric series variants

anchor.snippet: When the multiplication of the parameters is not commutative

annotations.note: `geom_sum_mul` and `Commute.geom_sumŌéé_mul` in `Mathlib/Algebra/Ring/GeomSum.lean` are stated for general (possibly non-commutative) rings.
